CorpusId:284452293.title: Tissue-Resident ILC2s Across Organs: Heterogeneity, Niche Crosstalk, and Shared Regulatory Circuits
CorpusId:284452293.abstract: Type 2 innate lymphoid cells (ILC2s) are an important source of innate cytokines that contribute to allergic inflammation. Recent studies have also suggested that ILC2s play a biological role in peripheral tissues such as lung, fat, intestine and skin, controlled by alarmins, neuropeptides and environmental factors. Furthermore, emerging studies have shown that tissues including the pancreas, uterus and meninges contain ILC2s that contribute to normal biological function. Here, we review recent
CorpusId:284452293.tldr: Recent studies on the physiological function of ILC2s in multiple tissues are reviewed, emphasizing their importance not only in type 2 immune responses, but also in maintaining biological homeostasis.

CorpusId:280847420.title: Natural killer cells in skin: a unique opportunity to better characterize the many facets of an overlooked secondary lymphoid organ
CorpusId:280847420.abstract: Natural killer (NK) cells are lymphoid-derived cells that play a critical role in bridging innate and adaptive immunity. Given their ability to recognize and directly kill targets possessing missing or altered self-proteins and to induce indirect killing via recruitment of adaptive immunity, they are in a unique position to modulate host immunologic responses. These complex immune sentinels typically circulate in the peripheral blood and/or reside in lymphoid tissues. As the largest organ, human
CorpusId:280847420.tldr: This review seeks to consolidate the currently available literature regarding NK cell and ILC skin homing and innate immune function in healthy vs. lesional human skin (including infection, inflammatory/autoimmune conditions, and cutaneous malignancy).

CorpusId:245219309.title: Cutaneous Type 2 Innate Lymphoid Cells Come in Distinct Flavors
CorpusId:245219309.abstract: In a new article published in JID Innovations, Nakatani-Kusakabe et al. (2021) show that type 2 innate lymphoid cells (ILC2s) in the skin of mice with IL-33 overexpression in keratinocytes are heterogeneous and consist of two distinct populations: skin-resident ILC2s and circulating ILC2s. They show that the circulating subset of skin ILC2s migrates to draining lymph nodes during hapten-induced cutaneous inflammation to potentially enhance the adaptive immune response.
CorpusId:245219309.tldr: It is shown that the circulating subset of skin ILC2s migrates to draining lymph nodes during hapten-induced cutaneous inflammation to potentially enhance the adaptive immune response.

CorpusId:249476233.title: Tissue-Specific Diversity of Group 2 Innate Lymphoid Cells in the Skin
CorpusId:249476233.abstract: Since the discovery of group 2 innate lymphoid cells (ILC2s), their developmental pathways, mechanisms of activation and regulation, and immunological roles in the steady state and in disease have been reported in various organs. ILC2s, which produce large amounts of IL-5 and IL-13 in response to tissue-derived factors and are essential in inducing and promoting allergic inflammation, have also been found to play multifaceted roles in maintaining tissue homeostasis. While T cells respond to fore
CorpusId:249476233.tldr: Overall, this review discusses the phenotypical and functional heterogeneity of skin ILCs reported in recent years and highlights future directions within the field of ILC biology.

CorpusId:283401098.title: Immunological control of skin development: from homeostasis to developmental pathologies
CorpusId:283401098.abstract: The skin serves not only as a physical barrier but also as a dynamic immune organ, where immune cells orchestrate tissue morphogenesis, homeostasis, and repair. Recent advances have revealed that immune cells play pivotal roles during skin development, guiding processes such as vascular formation, epidermal stratification, and hair follicle morphogenesis. In this review, we integrate fundamental mechanistic studies based on mouse models with key clinical observations from human diseases to compr
CorpusId:283401098.tldr: This review comprehensively elucidate the contributions of critical immune cell populations—including macrophages, Langerhans cells, dendritic cells, mast cells, mast cells, and innate lymphoid cells—to normal skin development and explores how dysregulation of immune cell functions leads to aberrant skin morphogenesis.

CorpusId:6252243.title: Innate lymphoid cells and the skin
CorpusId:6252243.abstract: Innate lymphoid cells are an emerging family of effector cells that contribute to lymphoid organogenesis, metabolism, tissue remodelling and protection against infections. They maintain homeostatic immunity at barrier surfaces such as lung, skin and gut (Nature 464:1367–1371, 2010, Nat Rev Immunol 13: 145–149, 2013). Several human and mouse studies suggest a role for innate lymphoid cells in inflammatory skin conditions including atopic eczema and psoriasis. Here we review the innate lymphoid ce
CorpusId:6252243.tldr: The innate lymphoid cell family is reviewed and their function in the skin and during inflammation is discussed, including their role in inflammatory skin conditions including atopic eczema and psoriasis.

CorpusId:207759460.title: Context Dependent Role of Type 2 Innate Lymphoid Cells in Allergic Skin Inflammation
CorpusId:207759460.abstract: The discovery of innate lymphoid cells (ILC) has profoundly influenced the understanding of innate and adaptive immune crosstalk in health and disease. ILC and T cells share developmental and functional characteristics such as the lineage-specifying transcription factors and effector cytokines, but importantly ILC do not display rearranged antigen-specific receptors. Similar to T cells ILC are subdivided into 3 different helper-like subtypes, namely ILC1-3, and a killer-like subtype comprising n
CorpusId:207759460.tldr: It is shown that lack of ILC2 in type 1 dominated contact hypersensitivity results in enhanced inflammation, suggesting a regulatory role of I LC2 in this context, and provides a concept of how ILC 2 may influence context dependent the mutual counterbalance between type I and type II immune responses in allergic skin diseases.

CorpusId:237838040.title: Monitoring Cellular Movement with Photoconvertible Fluorescent Protein and Single-Cell RNA Sequencing Reveals Cutaneous Group 2 Innate Lymphoid Cell Subtypes, Circulating ILC2 and Skin-Resident ILC2
CorpusId:237838040.abstract: We previously generated a transgenic mouse line expressing skin-specific IL-33 (IL33tg mice) and showed that IL-33 elicits group 2 innate lymphoid cell (ILC2)–dependent atopic dermatitis–like skin inflammation. ILC2s are believed to be tissue-resident cells under steady-state conditions, but the dynamics of ILC2 migration are not fully understood. We sorted ILC2s from the skin and draining lymph nodes of IL33tg mice and analyzed their transcriptomes using the single-cell RNA sequencing technique
CorpusId:237838040.tldr: The skin ILC2s turned out to be a heterogeneous cell population, similar to tissue-resident cells under steady-state conditions, and cutaneous local innate responses could transition to systemic type 2 responses by migrating the activated I LC2s from the skin into the draining lymph node.

CorpusId:6454819.title: Molecular Mechanisms of Cutaneous Inflammatory Disorder: Atopic Dermatitis
CorpusId:6454819.abstract: Atopic dermatitis (AD) is a multifactorial inflammatory skin disease resulting from interactions between genetic susceptibility and environmental factors. The pathogenesis of AD is poorly understood, and the treatment of recalcitrant AD is still challenging. There is accumulating evidence for new gene polymorphisms related to the epidermal barrier function and innate and adaptive immunity in patients with AD. Newly-found T cells and dendritic cell subsets, cytokines, chemokines and signaling pat
CorpusId:6454819.tldr: A review of the genetics, epigenetics, barrier dysfunction and immunological abnormalities in AD with a focus on updated molecular biology is presented.

CorpusId:275067877.title: Single-Cell Profiling Reveals Global Immune Responses During the Progression of Murine Epidermal Neoplasms
CorpusId:275067877.abstract: Simple Summary The immune system plays a crucial role in either fighting or supporting tumor growth. In this study, we analyzed immune cells in control skins and skin tumors using single-cell RNA sequencing. We identified 15 different immune cell types and observed significant changes in their composition during skin tumor progression. Macrophages became the dominant immune cells in tumors and acted as key regulators of immune interactions, while other immune cells, including Langerhans cells an
CorpusId:275067877.tldr: It is found that tumors activated specific signaling pathways, including Jak2/Stat3, and increased immune-suppressing cells like Tregs and M2 macrophages, which help tumors evade the immune system.

CorpusId:251103227.title: Eosinophils, but Not Type 2 Innate Lymphoid Cells, Are the Predominant Source of Interleukin 4 during the Innate Phase of Leishmania major Infection
CorpusId:251103227.abstract: Interleukin (IL)-4 plays a central role in the initiation of a type 2 T helper cell (Th2) response, which leads to non-healing and progressive infections with the protozoan parasite Leishmania (L.) major. Here, we tested the hypothesis that type 2 innate lymphoid cells (ILC2), which promote the development of Th2 cells, form an important source of IL-4 early after intradermal or subcutaneous L. major infection. Lineage-marker negative CD90.2+CD127+PD1− ILC2 were readily detectable in the ear or
CorpusId:251103227.tldr: It is concluded that eosinophils, but not ILC2, are a major innate source of IL-4 at the skin site of L. major infection.

CorpusId:266020020.title: Innate lymphoid cells: a new key player in atopic dermatitis
CorpusId:266020020.abstract: Atopic dermatitis (AD) is a common allergic inflammatory skin condition mainly caused by gene variants, immune disorders, and environmental risk factors. The T helper (Th) 2 immune response mediated by interleukin (IL)-4/13 is generally believed to be central in the pathogenesis of AD. It has been shown that innate lymphoid cells (ILCs) play a major effector cell role in the immune response in tissue homeostasis and inflammation and fascinating details about the interaction between innate and ad
CorpusId:266020020.tldr: The role of ILCs in skin homeostasis is summarized and highlights the signaling pathways in which I LCs may be involved in AD, thus providing valuable insights into the behavior of ILS in skinHomeostasis and inflammation, as well as new approaches to treating AD.

CorpusId:53036073.title: A Task Force Against Local Inflammation and Cancer: Lymphocyte Trafficking to and Within the Skin
CorpusId:53036073.abstract: The skin represents a specialized site for immune surveillance consisting of resident, inflammatory and memory populations of lymphocytes. The entry and retention of T cells, B cells, and ILCs is tightly regulated to facilitate detection of pathogens, inflammation and tumors cells. Loss of individual or multiple populations in the skin may break tolerance or increase susceptibility to tumor growth and spread. Studies have significantly advanced our understanding of the role of skin T cells and I
CorpusId:53036073.tldr: The core chemokine and adhesion molecules that regulate cell trafficking to and within the skin will be detailed, which may provide therapeutic avenues to promote tumor homing for a team of lymphocytes.

CorpusId:229519460.title: Innate lymphoid cells: The missing part of a puzzle in food allergy
CorpusId:229519460.venue: Allergy. European Journal of Allergy and Clinical Immunology
CorpusId:229519460.abstract: Food allergy is an increasingly prevalent disease driven by uncontrolled type 2 immune response. Currently, knowledge about the underlying mechanisms that initiate and promote the immune response to dietary allergens is limited. Patients with food allergy are commonly sensitized through the skin in their early life, later on developing allergy symptoms within the gastrointestinal tract. Food allergy results from a dysregulated type 2 response to food allergens, characterized by enhanced levels o
CorpusId:229519460.tldr: Recent evidence on how skin and gastrointestinal mucosal immune system contribute to both homeostasis and the development of food allergy, as well as the involvement of ILCs toward inflammatory processes and regulatory mechanisms are summarized.

CorpusId:248424757.title: Tissue Resident and Migratory Group 2 Innate Lymphoid Cells
CorpusId:248424757.abstract: Group 2 innate lymphoid cells (ILC2s) are present in both mouse and human mucosal and non-mucosal tissues and implicated in initiating type 2 inflammation. ILC2s are considered to be tissue resident cells that develop in the perinatal period and persist throughout life with minimal turning over in adulthood. However, recent studies in animal models have shown their ability to circulate between different organs during inflammation and their potential functions in the destined organs, suggesting t
CorpusId:248424757.tldr: Recent findings on ILC2 migration, including migration within, into and out of tissues during inflammation, are reviewed.

CorpusId:256843977.title: Innate lymphoid cell (ILC) subsets are enriched in the skin of patients with hidradenitis suppurativa
CorpusId:256843977.abstract: Hidradenitis suppurativa (HS) is a chronic relapsing inflammatory skin disease manifested as painful inflamed lesions including deep-seated nodules, abscesses and sinus tracts. The exact aetiology of HS is unclear. Recent evidence suggests that immune dysregulation plays a crucial role in pathogenesis and disease progression. Innate lymphoid cells (ILC) are a recently identified immune cell subset involved in mediating immunity, however their role in HS has not yet been investigated. Three disti
CorpusId:256843977.tldr: The findings suggest that Innate lymphoid cells (ILC) may participate in HS pathogenesis, and the frequencies of total ILC as well as ILC2 and ILC3 were significantly higher in non-lesional than lesional HS skin.
